summary: Comfortable without the ball under Tony Popovic. After thrashing the minnows early in Asian qualifying, they reverted to a secure 5-4-1 that ground out impressive wins over Japan and Saudi Arabia with ~30% possession. Compact and disciplined, but with real transition and set-piece threat — don't write them off in a tough Group D.
style: 5-4-1 out of possession — block central progression and encircle the opposition midfield, with the five-man line covering the flanks. Not purely passive: wing-backs jump and attackers press after backward/sideways passes. Transitions quickly, the wing-backs turning the 5-4-1 into a 3-4-3.

counterReliance.note: Soak up pressure in a 5-4-1, then break fast through the wing-backs

strengths: Disciplined, compact 5-4-1 that smothers central progression
Quick transitions via energetic wing-backs (Jordan Bos a key left outlet — 8 assists for Feyenoord)
Set-piece threat from towering defenders Souttar and Circati
Pace and power on the counter (Irankunda, Toure)
weaknesses: Cedes possession by design (~30%) — needs its moments to count
Lost target man Mitch Duke to injury before the tournament

keyPlayers.name: Mohamed Toure
keyPlayers.note: Steps in for the injured Duke — most non-penalty goals in the Championship since February (9, with two hat-tricks); power and clinical finishing on the break

keyPlayers.name: Jordan Bos
keyPlayers.note: The key attacking outlet — energy to get forward and link with the front line; 8 assists last season

keyPlayers.name: Nestory Irankunda
keyPlayers.note: Speedy, freakish ball-striker — three long-range goals in his Watford breakout

tactics: Hold a compact 5-4-1, encircle the midfield, then spring forward through the wing-backs into a 3-4-3; threaten from set pieces and on the counter. Looked dominant at times in qualifying — a genuine handful for anyone in this group.
sources: The Athletic — 2026 World Cup tactical guide, Group D (5 Jun 2026)
John Walker (@johnwalker_1986) — pre-tournament tactical scouting, May–Jun 2026
